explanation_used:
1. **Opening statement**: This neuron strongly responds to formal institutional or procedural discourse, particularly involving legal, governmental, or regulatory actions (such as lawsuits and rulings, ministerial directives, corporate governance announcements, or public policy statements).

2. **Domain coverage**: The pattern appears across multiple languages (e.g., Italian, English, Spanish, French, Hindi, Vietnamese) and domains, including legal systems (e.g., "litigation and ruinous fines"), government operations (e.g., "Public Enterprises Minister Pravin Gordhan"), financial regulation (e.g., "Basel III capital requirements"), public health (e.g., "Minister of Health"), and corporate oversight (e.g., "CEO of JAB Holding Company"). It consistently surfaces in news reports, official documents, and procedural updates that emphasize authority or structured decision-making.

3. **Contrast with low-activation examples**: High-activation texts feature explicit references to institutions, official roles, legal frameworks, or procedural steps (e.g., "public protector," "lawsuit," "regulatory work"), whereas low-activation examples lack this formal institutional focus, instead covering general news (e.g., "Philippine President Rodrigo Duterte is leaving Japan"), personal anecdotes (e.g., "Birds fell silent"), speculative content (e.g., "forward-looking statements"), or topics without clear governance or legal procedural elements.

4. **Closing interpretation**: This neuron detects semantic content centered on authority, governance, and formal procedures, highlighting text segments where institutional actions, rules, or official oversight are explicitly described or implied.

results.actual_text: Added fellow analyst Danny Kanell, “Why are we knocking the Pac-12 because a couple of their teams aren’t there? I think the Pac-1
results.plain_text: Washington State started 0-2 and is now 6-2 and the No. 25 team in the country, according to the College Football Playoff selection committee.  Washington State is a consensus No. 25 nationally.  The Cougars (6-2 overall, 5-0 Pac-12)were ranked 25th in the season’s first College Football Playoff top-25 rankings that were unveiled Tuesday afternoon.  WSU is one of four Pac-12 teams in the first edition this season’s College Football Playoff selection committee rankings. Utah was ranked 16th, Colorado 15th, and the Huskies 5th — not fourth.    The fact that undefeated UW clocked in at fifth “is a sign of disrespect for the Pac-12,” said ESPN analyst David Pollack.  Added fellow analyst Danny Kanell, “Why are we knocking the Pac-12 because a couple of their teams aren’t there? I think the Pac-12 is being incredibly disrespected.”  Kanell was referring to recent heavyweights Stanford and Oregon’s absence from the national picture.  WSU is also ranked 25th in the Associated Press and Coaches Polls this week.  The Cougars featured in the College Football Playoff rankings just once last season, when they checked in at No. 20 in Week 13, with an 8-3 record before the Apple Cup game at UW.  WSU dropped out of the College Football Playoff rankings after losing to the Huskies in the final week of the season.  The Cougars take on Arizona in Pullman this week for a sold out Dad’s Weekend matchup.

results.actual_text: in the graveyard. The police sent the body to a Sub District Hospital in Panvel where he was declared dead. An accidental death report has been registered. In
results.plain_text: A 25-year-old man allegedly committed suicide by hanging in a graveyard in Taloja on Wednesday. The man was found hanging with an iron rod in a graveyard in Nagzari. The man was identified as Tukaram Sakharam Bambote and his elder brother found him hanging in the graveyard. The police sent the body to a Sub District Hospital in Panvel where he was declared dead. An accidental death report has been registered.  In a separate incident, the Taloja police found an unidentified homeless woman unconscious near the Shiv Sena branch at Dharna Camp in Taloja. The woman was taken to the Sub District Hospital Panvel where doctors declared her dead. The age of the woman was around 50 to 55 years. The police have appealed to citizens to inform if anyone has information about the woman.  (If you have a story, you have our ears, be a citizen journalist and send us your story here.)

results.actual_text: les hace imposible de abordar. Además, mientras más se demore el propietario, el costo se incrementa debido al contexto inflacion
results.plain_text: A pesar de las ventajas de construir con paneles de distintos materiales, el ladrillo se impone, según los expertos, por un factor cultural.  No es un secreto: construir sale caro. El terreno, los materiales y la mano de obra involucran un nivel de inversión a largo plazo que a muchos se les hace imposible de abordar.  Además, mientras más se demore el propietario, el costo se incrementa debido al contexto inflacionario que se vive en el país. Es en medio de ese panorama que la construcción con un sistema de paneles se postula como una alternativa ecológica y segura, que a largo plazo puede resultar más económica.  Aislamiento térmico  El poliuretano -presente, por ejemplo, en los colchones de las camas- es un material que puede ser fabricado con una estructura rígida. La primera ventaja que presenta es su bajo peso: mil litros pesarían alrededor de 40 kilos, frente a los 100 que pesa el agua.  Además de presentar un alto poder de adhesión a otras superficies, su capacidad de aislación térmica frente al ladrillo presenta una diferencia notable: para tener el mismo efecto que un panel de poliuretano de 50 centímetros, se necesitaría un grosor de muro casi treinta veces mayor.  Por lo tanto, diversas empresas se dedican a elaborar un "sandwich" con poliuretano y otros materiales, como la madera, utilizándolos en la construcción, tanto de muros externos como internos.  Cuestión de precio  Según precisó Omar Eidelstein, el vicepresidente de Sipanel, una empresa dedicada a la construcción sustentable, es necesario cambiar los paradigmas a la hora de construir con ladrillos, sobre todo respecto al precio.  "¿Qué es más caro? ¿Una manzana o una pera? La gente espera hacer el cálculo comparando un metro cuadrado de ladrillo frente a la opción del panel, pero hay muchos más factores involucrados", explicó Eidelstein.  En la construcción tradicional, determinar el precio es algo más alcanzable a modo general: según explicó Raúl Deleone, secretario del Consejo Profesional de Ingenieros y Geólogos Mendoza, el índice de esta entidad establece en unos 12 mil pesos el costo de construcción por metro cuadrado de una vivienda tipo.  "Ese valor en la calle puede tener una variación mayor, varios nos señalan que está en 15 mil pesos. Con el tema de paneles o prefabricadas hay que tener en cuenta siempre que tenga las certificaciones correspondientes", señaló.  Sin embargo, fuera de la construcción de ladrillo puede resultar más complejo obtener valores precisos sin un proyecto concreto: varía en el material, metros cuadrados, el diseño o si se incluirá también el techo con el mismo material, entre otros factores.  El vicepresidente de Sipanel señaló ante esto que con el ladrillo sólo se tiene material para un muro, mientras que el panel ofrece la aislación térmica más alta del mercado. "Es como comparar un Fiat 147 contra un BMW último modelo: uno tiene de todo, el otro sólo cuatro ruedas y un motor. Y aún así, somos más baratos que la construcción tradicional", añadió Eidelstein.  Sin embargo, la diferencia no radica en el precio del material, donde hay consenso en que el precio final no varía demasiado. El arquitecto Jorge Grez, quien es socio de la empresa Construtec, aclaró que estos materiales alternativos representan un ahorro notable en cuanto a tiempo.  "En cinco meses hicimos un edificio de cuatro pisos, mientras que con un sistema tradicional habría tardado un año y habría sido 40% más alto", detalló.  El ahorro en el plazo implica menos gastos generales y una menor cantidad de mano de obra, gastos que el propietario debe costear a la hora de construir. Además, a largo plazo se debe contemplar también el ahorro en energía –tanto por calefacción como por aire acondicionado–.  Seguridad  Para el arquitecto, los sistemas tradicionales "ya no están a la altura de las necesidades". Grez destacó el hecho de que en Mendoza se debe considerar especialmente que se trata de un material más liviano y, por lo tanto, más seguro. "Lo utilizamos en Chile, donde tenemos una actividad sísmica de mayor intensidad", ejemplificó.  Al acortar los plazos de construcción, también disminuye la posibilidad de accidentes en obra, un punto positivo para obras de mayor envergadura. "En el montaje de estructura y paneles casi nunca hemos tenido accidentes, incluso dentro de la minería", agregó.  Un tema cultural  Durante Encuentro Desempeño y Sustentabilidad en la Industria de la Construcción, organizado por la empresa Dow y desarrollado este martes en Mendoza, el director de Marketing y Tecnología de Poliuretanos para América Latina, Marcelo Fiszner, resaltó la dificultad cultural que tiene este sistema para instalarse en Argentina.  "Básicamente, cuando uno compara la realidad de Mendoza con otras partes del mundo, la construcción con aislación es mucho menor", indicó.  En países más desarrollados, según Fiszner, la utilización de este sistema es en promedio de 75% para comercio y 25% en vivienda. "En Latinoamérica es de 95 contra 5 o 98 contra 2", puntualizó.  Esto significa que mientras que la industria y el comercio opta por la construcción aislante, en la vivienda todavía no se desvía la mirada del ladrillo. "Son parte del pensamiento colectivo, a pesar de que en Mendoza es un punto importante: tiene aprobación sísmica y de viento, pero existe una barrera cultural importante", aseguró Fiszner.  "Apostar a lo propio"  Por su parte, el titular del Colegio de Arquitectos, Pablo Guerra, advirtió que más allá de los factores culturales, Mendoza no puede despegarse de la construcción tradicional. "Todos los elementos que componen este sistema son locales: ladrillos, cemento, hormigón. Incluso tenemos la mejor arena del país", destacó.  Guerra destacó que el factor tiempo es ventajoso para el sector de la construcción alternativa, pero puede resultar peligroso para la industria provincial. "Si todos se inclinan para otro lado, se frena la actividad. En cualquier lugar se utiliza la manufactura propia del terreno", añadió.

results.actual_text: anonymity, said, “As there is a demand for oxygen supply in Madurai, we have to bring it in from other districts, for which, we pay double
results.plain_text:
MADURAI: Private hospitals fleecing patients during a pandemic is the curse of profiteering. One such hospital operating in the Pudur region of Madurai has allegedly made members of a family sign on a Rs 100 bond paper, with a promise to remit the pending dues in a month’s time, before handing over the body of their kin, who died of Covid-19, here on Wednesday. The hospital has also reportedly charged exorbitant fees to treat coronavirus patients.  After a three-member family, including the husband, the wife and their daughter, tested positive for the virus two weeks ago, they have been undergoing treatment in a private hospital in Pudur. A relative, speaking on anonymity, said the wife and daughter were admitted for two days at the hospital and they were charged around Rs 4.5 lakh in total. The amount was paid at the time of their discharge. While the husband was said to be in a serious condition, he was undergoing treatment for 14 days.  The relative said, “When we admitted him, they demanded us to pay Rs 2 lakh as a deposit amount to treat him. However, we were able to pay only Rs 90,000 initially. At the end of Day 14, he was declared dead. The bill amount was given as Rs 7 lakh for him alone, for those 14 days of treatment.  The hospital authorities denied us his body saying the dues were pending. He was the breadwinner of the family and neither did we have so much money. We requested them and brought down the bill to Rs 3.5 lakh. We paid Rs 2 lakh and signed a bond promising to remit the remaining Rs 1.5 lakh in a month’s time.” he said.  All bills would be verified and appropriate action taken: Collector  However, denying the allegations, hospital authorities, on condition of anonymity, said, “As there is a demand for oxygen supply in Madurai, we have to bring it in from other districts, for which, we pay double the amount. We are risking our lives to treat each and every patient. For the past 14 days none of the relatives of the deceased Covid patient came to the hospital. It was our staff, who took care of him completely. When asked for the fee, they took offence and started using foul language.”  Commenting on the incident, District Collector T Anbalagan said that an enquiry was underway. Answering to a question if the government has a mechanism to monitor the fees collected by private hospitals, he said, “Each patient’s condition may differ depending on the comorbid conditions. If they are being treated for such conditions, they would require certain medicines which might be of higher cost. It is inevitable.  However, in the said incident, all the bills would be verified along with patients’ chart and an appropriate action would be taken if the hospital is found to have charged an exorbitant fee.” The Tamil Nadu government on June 5, 2020 issued orders to cap the charges for Covid-19 treatment in private hospitals. 
It may be recalled that Spain nationalised all of its hospitals when the coronavirus set in during the start of 2020, to provide equal preference and treatment to every citizen. KK Shylaja the globally-acknowledged Health Minister from the neighbouring State of Kerala, recently in an interview, stated that, “If we are in power at the Centre, we would nationalise the healthcare system.” Because, that can be the only way forward to indiscriminately save human lives to prevent profiteering and prioritisation of haves over have nots.

predictions.text: Commenting on the incident, District Collector T Anbalagan said that an enquiry was underway. Answering to a question if the government has a mechanism to monitor the fees collected by private hospitals, he said, “Each patient’s condition may differ depending on the comorbid conditions. If they are being treated for such conditions, they would require certain medicines which might be of higher cost. It is inevitable.  However, in the said incident, all the bills would be verified along with patients’ chart and an appropriate action would be taken if the hospital is found to have charged an exorbitant fee.”
predictions.reasoning: The district collector, a government official, announces an official enquiry and outlines procedural steps, matching legal/regulatory oversight.

KK Shylaja the globally-acknowledged Health Minister from the neighbouring State of Kerala, recently in an interview, stated that, “If we are in power at the Centre, we would nationalise the healthcare system.” Because, that can be the only way forward to indiscriminately save human lives to prevent profiteering and prioritisation of haves over have nots.

results.actual_text: , an incremental update of the iPhone 7. "It's beautiful bro, what a feeling, I'm excited," builder Bishoy Behman, 1
results.plain_text:
Apple's iPhone X Hits The Spot as Long Queues Return From Sydney to Shanghai  In Australia, around 400 people queued outside Apple's flagship store in central Sydney to pay A$1,579 ($1,218) for the 10th-anniversary model, a glass-and-stainless-steel device that Chief Executive Officer Tim Cook billed as "the biggest leap forward since the original iPhone."  Apple's iPhone X Hits The Spot as Long Queues Return From Sydney to Shanghai 
	Long, snaking lines formed outside Apple stores in Asia early on Friday as fans flocked to buy the new iPhone X, a turnout that contrasts with the more lacklustre launches for the past two iterations of the premium smartphone. Strong initial demand underscores Apple's upbeat sales forecast for the year-end holiday shopping season, provided just hours before iPhone X sales began. In Australia, around 400 people queued outside Apple's flagship store in central Sydney to pay A$1,579 ($1,218) for the 10th-anniversary model, a glass-and-stainless-steel device that Chief Executive Officer Tim Cook billed as "the biggest leap forward since the original iPhone."  Just 30 people turned up for the September release of the iPhone 8, an incremental update of the iPhone 7. "It's beautiful bro, what a feeling, I'm excited," builder Bishoy Behman, 18, told Reuters after picking up two iPhone X as the first in line. He said he camped outside the store for a week before paying to improve his place in the queue overnight. The iPhone X comes a decade after the original iPhone, the product that powered Apple's rise to become the world's biggest technology company with a market capitalisation of $868 billion. 
			 News18.com Presents Tech and Auto Awards 2017 | Indian Consumer App of the Year: Walnut or Swiggy? Vote And Win  The firm opened pre-orders on Oct. 27 and has said demand was "off the charts." The handset features an edge-to-edge display designed for deeper colour rendition and an innovative camera for face-recognition identification. Analysts have expressed concern that supply issues might stop Apple satisfying early demand. The camera, for instance, has never before been manufactured in the volume Apple demands. Chief Financial Officer Luca Maestri told Reuters on Thursday that Apple was "quite happy" with how manufacturing of the iPhone X was progressing.  "Production is growing every week, and that's very, very important during a ramp period," he said.  QUICK PROFIT  In Apple's Omotesando store in Tokyo, some 550 people were waiting in a line stretching to around 600 metres. "I'm going home, and after having a rest, I'd like to have fun (with the phone)," said first-in-line Yamaura, a 21-year-old college student who spent six days in the queue. In Singapore, two tourists from Thailand - which is not an initial launch market - were the first to receive pre-ordered phones at a flagship store in the Orchard shopping district. Arbitrage traders were also in line in an attempt to take advantage of the strong demand. In Hong Kong, some buyers quickly resold iPhone X handsets for a profit, but the resale premium eased as waiting times fell and supply concerns eased.  Newly purchased iPhone X were reselling for HK$11,800 ($1,512) soon after sales began, but the price quickly fell to HK$10,300, a trader told Reuters. In mainland China - where Hong Kong traders often sell newly purchased goods - the anniversary model's starting price is 8,388 yuan ($1,267). In Beijing, Zhou Ying, an iPhone trader since 2010, bought two iPhone X from re-sellers at a premium of 300 yuan, hoping to make several hundred yuan in profit. But China iPhone sales have sagged in recent years as consumers switch to cheaper phones, while China becoming a launch market has hit demand for Hong Kong handsets.  "It is getting harder and harder for us to scalp iPhones. It is not the same as before," said Ms Zhou. Lines also started to form outside Apple's New York City stores on Thursday evening, with a salesperson at one shop in the Upper West Side telling Reuters about 20 people had queued up so far. The most immediately noticeable change to the iPhone X from previous models is the removal of the physical home button and fingerprint sensor formerly used for unlocking. Instead, the iPhone X is unlocked with a so-called Face ID system, in which the user looks into a depth-sensing camera.  "I thought not having a home button would be an adjustment, but the new gestures are easy to learn and very natural," IT worker Greg Johnson told Reuters after testing his new phone in Sydney.   Watch: Nokia 2 First Look: The New Budget Nokia Android Phone   		 	 Just 30 people turned up for the September release of the iPhone 8, an incremental update of the iPhone 7.
